scope_note: Section-level review covering all six kill-shot tests rendered in sections.json `part5`. No prior Cycle 1 review exists — Part 5 was created in the V6 restructure on 2026-04-07. This is effectively a first adversarial pass under Cycle 2 severity thresholds.
our_argument_summary: The dome's kill-shot page presents six binary tests with the rule 'if any single test confirms, globe is falsified; if any fails, dome is falsified.' Two are claimed confirmed (Sydney-Perth distance, Polaris altitude), three are pending (JFK-LHR flight asymmetry, SAA African cell, Eclipse 2026), and one (NMP drift) is failing on the dome's own tracker at 39.9% error. Our section argues (a) the confirmed tests are calibration not prediction, (b) the pending tests are non-discriminating because globe physics predicts the same outcome, and (c) the failing test is being papered over by tolerance widening. By the dome's own binary rule, one failing test should falsify the entire model — but the dome marks NMP as PASS via a widened tolerance.

holes_found.description: Test 2 (Polaris) states Polaris is 'offset by 0.66–0.74° (currently ~0.66°) and precessing.' WIN-065's detail_evidence says 'declination ~89.26°' → offset ~0.74°, describing the offset as the *current* value. The two sections therefore give slightly different current values for the same quantity in the same review. Dome defenders love this kind of internal inconsistency — they will quote both passages and ask which review is correct. For 2026, the polar offset is ~0.61–0.63° and shrinking (closest approach 2102 at ~0.46°). Both our figures are conservative, but they disagree with each other.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one value, cite a source (e.g., Hipparcos/Gaia proper motion or a USNO almanac lookup for 2026), and propagate it to WIN-065 and SEC-5.1 together. Recommended phrasing: 'Polaris is currently ~0.63° from the north celestial pole (2026 epoch; declination ~89.37°; shrinking toward a ~0.46° minimum near 2102).' Cross-ref WIN-065 and update its detail_evidence to match.

holes_found.description: Test 6 (NMP) asserts the dome's '50% tolerance was widened from a tighter value' without citing the dome page or commit that records the widening. A defender will demand the receipt. We have the analogous argument in WIN-043/WIN-059 but the kill-shot section stands alone here.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a footnote or inline link: cite the specific dome tracker page or OPEN-item that shows the tolerance change (e.g., `raw-text/` file name or a monitor.py diff), OR state 'the tolerance appears in monitor.py as a hardcoded constant of 0.50; the dome has not published a derivation for this threshold.' Either makes the claim checkable.

holes_found.description: Test 5 (Eclipse 2026) is a single paragraph that delegates entirely to Section 4.2. In a tab/standalone context — remember the kill-shot section is a dedicated tab — a motivated reader who clicks this tab first and finds a 4-sentence rebuttal for test 5 will conclude we don't have much to say about it. This is the reader-path attack surface the Phase 2 guidance warns about.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand the Test 5 block by at least two paragraphs with the core kill-shot-relevant points inline: (1) the −17 to −21 nT Ebro prediction, (2) the Chapman mechanism's 5–25 nT prediction range envelope that already covers the dome's value, (3) the fact that the dome does not derive the Ebro number from dome geometry (pointing at the relevant formula or lack thereof), and (4) the geographic-path issue (Besselian ephemerides). Keep the 'full analysis in Section 4.2' pointer, but the standalone reader must not be left with a skim-path vulnerability.

holes_found.description: Test 4 (SAA African cell) criticises the dome for using 'extrapolation not geometry' but does not point the reader at the existing mainstream numerical forecasts to confirm the non-discriminating claim. We cite Aubert (2015) as '1.46 ± 0.4 μT surface field decrease by 2065' — that's a whole-field surface decrease, not a localized African cell forecast. A dome defender will say 'your citation is about a global trend over 40 years, not my specific 2028 cell value.' The Aubert citation is not doing the work we imply it does.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace Aubert 2015 with a source that specifically forecasts African SAA cell intensity for 2026–2028 (e.g., IGRF-14 forward extrapolation or Terra-Nova et al. 2017 regional analysis), or (b) change the framing to 'mainstream models all predict continued weakening of the SAA region; Aubert 2015 forecasts a broad surface-field decrease of ~1.46 μT by 2065, which is consistent with the dome's localized extrapolation and does not discriminate.' The current wording overstates what Aubert says.

holes_found.description: Test 1 (Sydney-Perth) contains the key fact that the dome's own MDS road-distance scaffold gives 3,893 km while the Finsler formula gives 4,352 km, a 460 km self-disagreement. This is a devastating point and deserves to be much more prominent — currently it's the second-to-last sentence of the last bullet in Problem 3, buried in a long paragraph about Sydney-Buenos Aires. A skim reader will miss it entirely.
holes_found.recommendation: Promote the self-disagreement into its own labelled sub-bullet or pull-quote, e.g., 'Problem 4 — the dome's own coordinate system disagrees with itself: MDS-on-roads → 3,893 km; V13 Finsler → 4,352 km; 460 km gap on the same city pair.' This is the strongest single sentence in the whole section and is currently buried.

holes_found.description: Test 3 (JFK-LHR) cites 'Williams, 2016, Environ. Res. Lett.' for winter ~62 min vs summer ~51 min asymmetry. Williams (2016) is actually primarily about climate-change-driven jet stream strengthening and *eastbound* transatlantic flight time reductions — the specific winter/summer minute values I cannot independently confirm without fetching the paper. If those specific numbers are pulled from a secondary source or approximated, we should either verify against the paper directly or soften the claim to 'seasonal asymmetry of roughly 50–65 minutes, larger in winter, as documented in Williams (2016) and airline schedule data.'
holes_found.recommendation: Fetch the Williams 2016 paper and verify the specific 62/51 minute figures. If they came from a secondary source, cite that source. If they are round-number approximations from schedule data, say so.

holes_found.description: Test 3's 'The framing problem' paragraph ends 'The wind _is_ the explanation.' This is rhetorically satisfying but glides past the dome defender's best counter: 'Fine — then show me a formula in any mainstream aeronautics textbook that predicts the 55–75 minute asymmetry from first principles without calibration to measured wind speeds.' The honest answer is that both models rely on measured/modeled wind fields. We should preempt that counter.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence: 'The jet stream is measured in situ (GPS dropsondes, satellite wind retrievals, reanalysis models like ERA5) independently of flight times; the flight-time asymmetry is then derived from those measured winds. The dome's aetheric slipstream is not measured independently anywhere — it is defined as whatever produces the asymmetry, which is unfalsifiable.'

holes_found.description: Throughout the section, the framing rule ('if any single test fails, dome is falsified') is stated once in the opening paragraph but never invoked again at the conclusion of the NMP test. The section should close on this asymmetry: the dome's own rule, applied to its own failing test, already falsifies the dome. We currently imply this but don't say it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a short closing paragraph after Test 6: 'By the dome's own binary rule, a single failing test falsifies the model. Test 6 (NMP drift) is currently failing at 39.9% error on the dome's own tracker, even with a tolerance widened to 50%. By the rule as stated, the dome should be considered falsified by its own scoring criteria. The fact that the site still marks the NMP prediction as PASS is itself the strongest evidence that the binary-test framing is not being enforced symmetrically.'

stronger_arguments.description: Against Test 1 (Sydney-Perth): point out that the exact same V13 Finsler formula, applied to the reverse test — Sydney to Auckland, a short trans-Tasman route with no railway to calibrate against — either produces large errors or is not reported on the dome site. Ask why. The absence of reverse tests on short, well-measured routes is itself evidence of selective publication.
sources: The dome's own coordinate page (raw-text/) listing which city pairs are tested
Flight distance Sydney-Auckland: ~2,157 km (well-measured great circle)

stronger_arguments.description: Against Test 2 (Polaris): the binary rule invoked by the dome says 'if any test confirms, globe is falsified.' But a +0.27° excess at 35.9°N would only falsify the globe if no other globe-consistent mechanism could produce it. Atmospheric refraction, observer latitude measurement error, polar offset, and time-of-observation all produce signals at or above 0.27°. The signal is smaller than the known noise floor, so even if it were real and measured, it cannot falsify anything.
sources: Kovalevsky & Seidelmann, Fundamentals of Astrometry (2004) — atmospheric refraction at moderate altitudes

stronger_arguments.description: Against Test 6 (NMP): connect directly to the 'asymmetric scorecard' theme from Section 4. The dome marks this as PASS with 39.9% error but marks minor globe mismatches as FAIL with <10% error on other pages. Quantify the asymmetry.

kernel_of_truth.description: The binary-test framing is an honest methodological commitment. Most pseudoscientific models avoid binary commitments entirely (adaptive tolerances, vague predictions, post-hoc rationalization). The dome at least publishes six specific tests with specific numerical thresholds and commits in writing that a single failure falsifies the model. That's more epistemic hygiene than most fringe physics offers. The author deserves credit for making the commitment — even if the enforcement is asymmetric in practice.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Epistemic commitment only matters if it's honored. The NMP test is currently failing by the dome's own numerics, and the dome has widened tolerances to avoid calling it a failure. A binary rule that is not enforced when results are inconvenient is a one-way ratchet: confirmations count, failures get reclassified. This is worse than having no binary rule, because it gives the reader a false impression of falsifiability.

cross_section_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Polaris polar offset value disagreement between SEC-5.1 ('0.66–0.74°, currently ~0.66°') and WIN-065 detail_evidence ('declination ~89.26°' → 0.74°). Must be reconciled — these appear in the same published review and a defender reading both will flag it.

deeper_analysis:
The kill-shot tab is structurally load-bearing. The dome's home page presents six tests as the decisive experiments, and a reader visiting that tab first — or an AI pasting the dome URL and asking 'is this debunk any good?' — will compare the dome's six tests directly against our six rebuttals in a head-to-head. That means each rebuttal must stand on its own without requiring the reader to have absorbed Sections 1–4 first. Currently: Tests 1, 3, 4, and 6 do stand on their own (they're long and self-contained). Test 2 is adequate but has the Polaris polar offset number disagreement with WIN-065. Test 5 does not stand on its own — it is four sentences, delegates everything to Section 4.2, and will read as evasive to a skim reader. Fixing Test 5 is the highest-priority structural fix here. The second priority is promoting the MDS-vs-Finsler self-disagreement in Test 1 into its own labelled problem, because that is the single most rhetorically powerful factual claim in the entire kill-shot section and is currently buried in the Sydney-Buenos Aires paragraph. The third priority is adding a closing paragraph that invokes the dome's own binary rule against the failing NMP test — that rhetorical move costs one paragraph and dramatically increases the section's standalone punch.

Advocate-mode test (informal, not yet Cycle 3+): a genuinely charitable dome defender who accepts the six methodological principles from Section Evaluation Guide could still argue: (1) 'calibration vs prediction is a continuum — the Indian Pacific railway distance may have been input, but the coordinate system is tested against many other pairs, and the full Finsler model has real predictive content.' Our rebuttal should acknowledge this and point to the *absence* of published reverse tests (Sydney-Auckland, Perth-Adelaide, Brisbane-Cairns). (2) 'The NMP prediction is framed as a range, and 39.9% error within a 50% tolerance is by definition a pass under published rules — calling it a fail requires our own tolerance choice, which is a form of goalpost-moving.' Our rebuttal: the 50% tolerance is itself arbitrary and not derived from anything; if tolerances are set after seeing the data they are not honest. (3) 'The globe also relies on unmeasured mechanisms — you can't point to an aether to find the jet stream; you infer it from wind models.' Our rebuttal: wind fields are measured directly by dropsondes, radiosondes, satellite retrievals, and aircraft in-situ reporting; they are not back-derived from the flight times they supposedly explain. That's an independently testable mechanism; aetheric slipstream is not.

citations_verified: Finlay et al. 2020 CHAOS-7 DOI 10.1186/s40623-020-01288-x — correct DOI, correct paper
Williams 2016 DOI 10.1088/1748-9326/11/2/024008 — valid DOI in Environmental Research Letters; paper is 'Transatlantic flight times and climate change'
Indian Pacific railway distance 4,352 km — matches published Australasian Railway Association figures
Livermore et al. 2020 Nature Geoscience — real paper on 1999–2005 NMP acceleration via hydromagnetic jet
WMM2025 and IGRF-14 release timing (late 2024) — correct
citations_failed: Aubert 2015 '1.46 ± 0.4 μT surface field decrease by 2065' — number may be correct but the Aubert forecast is for global surface field, not the localized African SAA cell value the paragraph is arguing about. The citation is used in a way that overstates what Aubert actually predicts. See holes_found moderate #4.
citations_unchecked: Williams 2016 specific winter/summer asymmetry values (62 min / 51 min) — need to verify these are the actual numbers in the paper versus a secondary source
Terra-Nova et al. 2017 PNAS (cited briefly under Test 4) — paper exists but specific claim about SAA bifurcation identification timing not independently checked in this review
Polaris current polar offset for 2026 epoch — value disagreement between SEC-5.1 and WIN-065 noted as a hole
